answers.question: List of scenarios
answers.evidence: The scenarios are summarized in the table below: Objective | Orderly 1.5° C | Too Little Too Late >2° C | Hothouse >3° C
answers.notes: Three scenarios are used, developed by the Financial Services Council (FSC) Climate Working Group for the New Zealand financial services sector. Each scenario maps to specific IPCC SSPs, NGFS, IEA, NIWA RCPs, and CCC pathways.
answers.ani_notes: Orderly -1.5oC  Too litltle too late (2oC ) Hothouse (3oC)

answers.question: Global temperature outcome by 2100
answers.answer: Three temperature outcomes by 2100 are described: (1) Orderly scenario: global +1.4°C (min 1.0, max 1.8), domestic NZ +0.7°C (min 0.4, max 1.3); (2) Too Little Too Late scenario: global +2.7°C (min 2.1, max 3.5), domestic NZ +1.4°C (min 0.7, max 2.2); (3) Hothouse scenario: global +4.4°C (min 3.3, max 5.7), domestic NZ +3.0°C (min 2.0, max 4.6). No temperature overshoot is explicitly mentioned.
answers.evidence: Average temperature increase by 2100: • Domestic: +0.7°C by 2100 (min 0.4, max 1.3) • Global: +1.4° C by 2100 (min 1.0, max 1.8) [Orderly] Average temperature increase by 2100: • Domestic: +1.4°C by 2100 (min 0.7, max 2.2) • Global: +2.7° C by 2100 (min 2.1, max 3.5) [Too Little Too Late] Average temperature increase by 2100: • Domestic: +3.0°C by 2100 (min 2.0, max 4.6) • Global: +4.4° C by 2100 (min 3.3, max 5.7) [Hothouse]
answers.notes: Both global and New Zealand domestic temperature outcomes are provided for each scenario. The scenario labels also indicate >2°C and >3°C thresholds.
answers.ani_notes: • Domestic: +0.7°C by 2100 (min 0.4, max 1.3) • Global: +1.4° C by 2100 (min 1.0, max 1.8) Domestic: +1.4°C by 2100 (min 0.7, max 2.2) • Global: +2.7° C by 2100 (min 2.1, max 3.5) Domestic: +3.0°C by 2100 (min 2.0, max 4.6) • Global: +4.4° C by 2100 (min 3.3, max 5.7)

answers.question: Scenario provider
answers.answer: Multiple providers are explicitly named: FSC (Financial Services Council) and EY developed the overarching scenario narratives for the New Zealand financial services sector. Underlying data sources include IPCC, NGFS (Network for Greening the Financial System), IEA (International Energy Agency), NIWA (National Institute of Water and Atmospheric Research), and CCC (Climate Change Commission).
answers.evidence: Chubb Life was a member of the FSC's Climate Working Group, which was involved in the development of the climate scenario narratives for the financial services sector. [...] 6 Climate Scenario Narratives for the Financial Services Sector, EY and FSC, page 29
answers.location_hint: Appendix A: Scenario Narratives, p.15; footnotes p.9
answers.notes: The primary scenario narrative provider is EY and FSC (Financial Services Council). The underlying scenario data comes from IPCC, NGFS, IEA, NIWA, and CCC.
answers.ani_notes: Global climate and socio-economic parameters: IPCC SSP1-1.9, IPCC SSP2-4.5, IPCC SSP5-8.5 Global energy and emission pathway parameters: NGFS Net Zero 2050 IEA Net Zero Emissions by 2050, NGFS NDCs IEA APS, NGFS Current Policies IEA STEPS New Zealand-specific climate parameters: NIWA RCP2.6, NIWA RCP4.5, NIWA RCP 8.5 New Zealand-specific transition pathway parameters: CCC ‘Tailwinds’, CCC ‘Headwinds’, CCC ‘Current Policy Reference’

answers.question: Pick-and-mix of SSPs and RCPs
answers.evidence: Global climate and socio-economic parameters: IPCC SSP1-1.9 / IPCC SSP2-4.5 / IPCC SSP5-8.5 [...] New Zealand-specific climate parameters: NIWA RCP2.6 / NIWA RCP4.5 / NIWA RCP 8.5
answers.notes: The document explicitly lists both SSP identifiers (IPCC SSP1-1.9, SSP2-4.5, SSP5-8.5) and RCP identifiers (NIWA RCP2.6, RCP4.5, RCP8.5) used together within the same scenario framework. SSPs and RCPs are from different scenario generations (IPCC AR6 vs AR5) and are not from the same internally consistent scenario family.
answers.ani_notes: Yes - they have mixed and picked

answers.question: Customised scenarios
answers.evidence: In addition to the industry scenarios, our Risk and Compliance team led internal research to deepen our understanding of our alternative futures, including the impacts specific to New Zealand in terms of both physical and transitional impacts. We also considered factors specific to Chubb Life, including our value chain (as described above) and the distribution of our customers across New Zealand. We added details by setting out and testing broad assumptions about our business, including our likely distribution models and the likely behaviour of our closest competitors.
answers.notes: The scenarios were adapted for the New Zealand financial services sector context (via FSC/EY), and further customised by Chubb Life with NZ-specific parameters (NIWA RCPs, CCC pathways) and company-specific assumptions. The document also notes additional research was undertaken "from a life insurance perspective."
answers.ani_notes: Via our Financial Services Council membership, we participated in the preparation of industry scenarios In addition to the industry scenarios, our Risk and Compliance team led internal research to deepen our understanding of our alternative futures, including the impacts specific to New Zealand in terms of both physical and transitional impacts Chubb Life was a member of the FSC’s Climate Working Group, which was involved in the development of the climate scenario narratives for the financial services sector.

answers.question: Co-produced climate scenarios
answers.evidence: Chubb Life was a member of the FSC's Climate Working Group, which was involved in the development of the climate scenario narratives for the financial services sector. The use of sectoral scenarios allows consumers to more easily compare disclosures. The process to determine the scenario narratives followed five stages: 1. Engagement of stakeholders, including the establishment of the working group and the Steering Committee. [...] 4. Selected the scenarios and identified risks, opportunities and their pathways. Work was undertaken with the members to identify the key climate-related risks, and the impacts of these risks under different scenarios.
answers.notes: The scenario development process involved stakeholder engagement, a working group, and a Steering Committee. Chubb Life participated as a member of the FSC's Climate Working Group in the co-development of the sector narratives. Internal workshops were also held with the Climate Working Group.
answers.ani_notes: Yes - co produced with industry sector

answers.question: Baseline scenarios
answers.notes: The document does not describe a separate baseline or reference scenario against which the three climate scenarios are compared. The three scenarios (Orderly, Too Little Too Late, Hothouse) are presented as alternative futures, not compared against a defined baseline. The Hothouse scenario could be considered a "current policies" reference, but it is not explicitly labelled as a baseline.

answers.question: Model used
answers.evidence: Global: NGFS Net Zero by 2050 25.9 BtCO2e by 2050, -294.82 MtCO2e by 2050 using GCAM5.3+ (NGFS) [...] GCAM: Global Change Analysis Model
answers.notes: GCAM5.3+ is the only specific model named in the document. It is used for NGFS emissions pathway calculations. No other IAMs, climate models, or macroeconomic models are explicitly named.
answers.ani_notes: None (but GCAM5.3+ is mentioned for emissions pathway calculations, Q. check?)

answers.question: Damage from acute physical risk considered
answers.evidence: We are proactively controlling our corporate operations' exposure to acute physical risks from climate change, such as flooding and landslides, by ensuring we have appropriate ways of working including business continuity practices and support for our teams. [...] Flooding and other acute physical risks could lead to increased insurance costs for Chubb Life and potential costs for retreat/adaptation, even where Chubb Life facilities are not directly impacted. [...] GDP (GDP % change due to chronic physical risk, acute impacts are excluded from this figure and would further negatively impact GDP)
answers.location_hint: Strategy section, p.10; Appendix A, p.16
answers.notes: The document explicitly discusses acute physical risks (flooding, landslides, extreme weather) and their potential financial impacts on Chubb Life. The scenario table notes that GDP figures exclude acute impacts, acknowledging they would "further negatively impact GDP." However, no specific quantitative damage/loss estimates from acute events are provided.

answers.question: Non-temperature variables in scenarios
answer: GHG emissions pathway (domestic and global MtCO2e)
answers.evidence: Carbon price: • Domestic ETS: NZ$140 in 2030, NZ$250 in 2050 • Global: US$124 in 2030, US$400 in 2050 [...] Percent of renewable electricity of total electricity produced [...] Percent of renewable energy of total energy produced [...] NZ GDP: NZ$330 billion (-0.5%) in 2030 [...] Global population: 8 billion by 2030 [...] Physical risks arising from climate change can also be chronic, due to longer term shifts in climate patterns. These include persistent high temperatures, rising sea levels, and changes in rainfall.
answers.location_hint: Appendix A: Scenario Narratives, pp.15-16; Strategy section, p.10
answers.notes: The scenario table explicitly includes carbon price, emissions pathways, renewable energy percentages, GDP, and population. Sea level rise, rainfall, and flooding are discussed as physical risk variables in the narrative sections.

answers.question: Time frames
answers.answer: Short-term: 1–3 years; Medium-term: 5–10 years; Long-term: 30+ years. Rationale is provided: short to medium term aligns with strategic planning horizons (~5 years); long-term is not linked to strategic planning or capital deployment but aligns with the long-term nature of life insurance policies written.
answers.evidence: For each scenario we assessed potential short-term (1-3 years), medium term (5-10 years), and long term (30+ years) impacts. The short to medium term time frames align with our strategic planning horizons (of approximately five years). The long-term timeframe is not linked to our strategic planning or capital deployment, but provides an interesting opportunity for us to consider risks and opportunities in a timeframe that aligns with the long term nature of the life insurance policies we write.
answers.notes: Rationale is explicitly provided for all three time horizons.
answers.ani_notes: Short-term (1-3 years) Medium-term (5-10 years) Long-term (+30 years) impact

answers.question: Justification for scenario/model choice
answers.evidence: The sector narratives are supported by robust rationale for their inclusion. The Orderly and Hothouse scenarios are commonly used in relevant industries, and have been used in other New Zealand sectoral analyses. The Too Little Too Late scenario is not commonly used, but is considered more realistic than the "Disorderly" scenario. In order to further satisfy ourselves that the scenarios were relevant and appropriate, we reviewed the narratives against the scenario quality check factors set out in the XRB's Staff Guidance on Scenario development, as well as the express requirements of the Climate Standards. We also undertook additional research relating to climate change and its impacts from a life insurance perspective, to ensure that our understanding of the impacts on human health and life was robust. [...] The chosen scenarios ensure we consider a wide range of possible risks and opportunities that could emerge, and allow us to assess how similar risks could emerge in different ways in different scenarios.
answers.notes: The document provides explicit justification for the choice of all three scenarios, including: common industry use, NZ sectoral precedent, realism compared to alternatives, alignment with XRB guidance, and suitability for the life insurance context.
answers.ani_notes: The sector narratives are supported by robust rationale for their inclusion. The Orderly and Hothouse scenarios are commonly used in relevant industries, and have been used in other New Zealand sectoral analyses. The Too Little Too Late scenario is not commonly used, but is considered more realistic than the “Disorderly” scenario. In order to further satisfy ourselves that the scenarios were relevant and appropriate, we reviewed the narratives against the scenario quality check factors set out in the XRB’s Staff Guidance on Scenario development, as well as the express requirements of the Climate Standards. We also undertook additional research relating to climate change and its impacts from a life insurance perspective, to ensure that our understanding of the impacts on human health and life was robust. See the References at the end of this report for a list of the New Zealand-specific resources used in conducting our scenario analysis.
